RT by @OsintTV: OSINT ASSESSMENT:

Operation Sindoor left Pakistan with a serious deep scar. They claimed strikes deep inside India but failed to produce a single credible satellite image to support those claims.

Now comes PRSC-EO3.

Looks like the real mission is not surveillance, but content creation for the next press conference. If Op Sindoor 2.0 ever happens, expect plenty of fake satellite imagery for domestic consumption, regardless of what actually happened on the ground.

Some countries build capabilities for warfare. Others build capabilities to explain away its outcome.

Interestingly, PRSC-EO3 was launched on April 25, 2026, with the help of China's Taiyuan Satellite Launch Center, barely a year after Operation Sindoor exposed the credibility gap in Pakistan's battlefield claims.

R to @NetramDefence: An Enduring Legacy

Though India is gradually replacing liquid-fueled Prithvi variants with faster-reacting, solid-fueled systems such as Prahaar and Pralay, the missile remains a landmark achievement. Produced in series by Bharat Dynamics Limited and deployed across multiple regiments, Prithvi proved that India could master complex missile technology and laid the foundation for the nation’s rise as a major missile power.

An Enduring Legacy

Though India is gradually replacing liquid-fueled Prithvi variants with faster-reacting, solid-fueled systems such as Prahaar and Pralay, the missile remains a landmark achievement. tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Beyond Strike Missions

Prithvi’s legacy extends far beyond offensive operations. Its airframe served as the basis for the Prithvi Air Defence (PAD) interceptor, a critical component of India’s Ballistic Missile Defence programme. Knowledge gained from Prithvi also contributed directly to the development of the Agni missile family.

Knowledge gained from Prithvi also contributed directly to the development of the Agni missile family.

tweets.title:
RT by @OsintTV: How the Pahalgam Terrorists Were Sheltered Before the Massacre

A local man reportedly saw three armed men—later identified as the terrorists behind the Pahalgam massacre—enter the dhok of ponywallah Parvaiz Ahmad Jothar on 21 April 2025, a day before the attack. He saw them with Parvaiz and his uncle Bashir Ahmad Jothar, and then again at Baisaran Meadows on the afternoon of 22 April.

The witness stated that the terrorists confronted him but spared his life after he successfully recited the Kalma on their orders.

On the evening of 21 April, Bashir encountered the three armed terrorists in a forested area. The men requested shelter and food in the name of Allah. Bashir then brought them to the dhok of his nephew Parvaiz, where they were welcomed, given water, tea, and dinner.

The terrorists spoke about jihad and fighting for the freedom of Kashmiri Muslims. Their Urdu, spoken with a Punjabi accent, made it clear that they were not local Kashmiris. Parvaiz hid the three men under blankets and bedding inside the dhok while food and refreshments were arranged for them.

The terrorists stayed for nearly five hours. Before leaving around 10 p.m., they packed rotis, vegetables, spices, utensils, blankets, and a tarpaulin sheet for their onward movement. They also handed over ₹3,000.

The following day, despite  seeing the same terrorists again near Baisaran Meadows, both Parvaiz and Bashir continued their routine work of taking tourists on ponies and did not raise any alarm. They remained in the area until around 1 p.m. before returning downhill.

Later, blankets and a tarpaulin sheet recovered from Baisaran helped establish the sequence of events preceding the massacre. At the attack site, 38 empty cartridges were recovered, revealing the intensity of the firing carried out on tourists.

Forensic examination further established that the weapons used in the Pahalgam massacre were the same weapons later recovered from terrorists neutralised in Operation Mahadev. The analysis also linked one of those weapons—an M-4 carbine—to the earlier Gagangir tunnel attack in Ganderbal, indicating a common terror network behind both attacks.

The emerging picture shows that the terrorists were provided shelter, food, supplies, and time to prepare before carrying out one of the most horrific attacks on civilians in recent memory.

The terrorists spoke about jihad and fighting for the freedom of Kashmiri Muslims. Their Urdu, spoken with a Punjabi accent, made it clear that they were not local Kashmiris. Parvaiz hid the three men under blankets and bedding inside the dhok while food and refreshments were arranged for them.

The terrorists stayed for nearly five hours. Before leaving around 10 p.m., they packed rotis, vegetables, spices, utensils, blankets, and a tarpaulin sheet for their onward movement. They also handed over ₹3,000.

The following day, despite seeing the same terrorists again near Baisaran Meadows, both Parvaiz and Bashir continued their routine work of taking tourists on ponies and did not raise any alarm. They remained in the area until around 1 p.m. before returning downhill.

Later, blankets and a tarpaulin sheet recovered from Baisaran helped establish the sequence of events preceding the massacre. At the attack site, 38 empty cartridges were recovered, revealing the intensity of the firing carried out on tourists.

Forensic examination further established that the weapons used in the Pahalgam massacre were the same weapons later recovered from terrorists neutralised in Operation Mahadev. The analysis also linked one of those weapons—an M-4 carbine—to the earlier Gagangir tunnel attack in Ganderbal, indicating a common terror network behind both attacks.

The emerging picture shows that the terrorists were provided shelter, food, supplies, and time to prepare before carrying out one of the most horrific attacks on civilians in recent memory.
